full_text:
Pablo Siliceo

Topological data analysis identifies conserved multiscale geometrical features associated with immune exhaustion in squamous cell carcinomas

Pablo Siliceo1, Cruz Osuna1, Luis Raul Figueroa Martínez2, Haydeé Peruyero2, Sonia del Marro6, Eugenio Azpeitia2, Azucena Ocampo9, Sara Frías1,5, Leonard Harris6, Ramón García-Escudero6, Joel Greenberger4, Eunike Velleuer8, Anniina Färkkilä3, Alfredo Rodríguez1*

1Departamento de Medicina Genómica y Toxicología Ambiental, Instituto de Investigaciones, Biomédicas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México. pablosiliceo@iibiomedicas.unam.mx 2Centro de Ciencias Matemáticas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Morelia, Mexico. 3Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland. 4University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, United States of America. 5Laboratorio de Citogenética, Instituto Nacional de Pediatría, México. 6Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States. 7Molecular and Translational Oncology Unit, Centro de Investigaciones Energéticas, Medioambientales y Tecnológicas (CIEMAT), Avda. Complutense 40, 28040, Madrid, Spain. 8Centre for Child and Adolescent Health, HELIOS Klinikum, 47805 Krefeld, Germany. 9Instituto Estatal de Cancerología "Dr. Arturo Beltrán Ortega", Guerrero, México. *Corresponding author

Abstract

Spatial omics have paved the way for the identification of native targetable interactions in the tumour microenvironment (TME) by generating complex multidimensional tissue atlases. However, data interpretation heavily relies on the definition of cellular niches through cell type proximity and composition, ignoring key dimensions of tissue architecture, like the geometrical organization of cells.

The use of topological methods in biological datasets has enabled non-supervised identification of higher-dimensional features that more accurately describe and predict data shape and connectivity. A fine example is the implementation of topological data analysis (TDA) by Rips complex formation, a method grounded in the progressive generation of geometrical networks in space, to identify repeating structural motifs in data. Nonetheless, its application in spatial biology is rather scant.

We implemented neighbourhood analysis and TDA in data from multiplexed immunofluorescent whole-section images of squamous cell carcinomas from patients, as well as chemical (4NQO) and spontaneous (conditional p53 KO) carcinogenesis mouse models. Unlike neighbourhood analysis alone, we found niche-specific topological footprints encoding for multiscale geometrical features associated with disease progression and myeloid-mediated T cell exhaustion.

In this work we demonstrate how TDA is a useful tool for the exploration, visualization and analysis of spatial networks in the TME.
